chapters.title: A Journey through States of Water

concepts.title: States of Water
concepts.definition: Water exists in three different states: solid (ice), liquid (water), and gaseous (water vapour). Each state has distinct properties: ice (solid) retains its shape and does not flow or spread; water (liquid) flows, spreads, and takes the shape of its container while keeping its volume constant; water vapour (gas) is invisible and exhibits the property of spreading out in the entire available space.
examples: Ice cubes or ice in a freezer (solid state).
Water in a glass, milk, oil, or lemonade (liquid state).
Invisible water vapour in the air around us (gaseous state).

concepts.title: Evaporation
concepts.definition: The process of conversion of water into its vapour state is called evaporation. This process takes place continuously, even at room temperature.
examples: Drying of wet clothes on a line.
Water disappearing from puddles in a playground.
Water sprinkled on a hot pan (like making a dosa) turning into steam.
Mopped floor drying up after some time.
Hand sanitiser disappearing as you rub it on your hands.

concepts.title: Factors Affecting Evaporation
concepts.definition: The rate of evaporation is affected by several conditions: it is faster with a larger exposed surface area, higher temperature (like in sunlight), and increased air movement (wind). Conversely, higher humidity—which is the amount of water vapour present in the air—makes evaporation slower.
examples: Water evaporates faster from a wide plate than from a small bottle cap (Activity 8.7).
Clothes dry faster on a hot sunny day or a windy day than on a cold or rainy day.
Sweat on our body evaporates faster when we sit under a fan.
Clothes dry slowly on a rainy day when the air is more humid.

concepts.title: Humidity
concepts.definition: Humidity is the amount of water vapour present in the air. High humidity, common on rainy days, slows down the process of evaporation.
examples: The air feeling 'heavy' or clothes taking longer to dry on a rainy day.
Humidity data being reported in newspapers to describe daily weather patterns.

concepts.title: Condensation
concepts.definition: The process of conversion of water vapour into its liquid state is called condensation. This occurs when water vapour comes in contact with a cold surface.
examples: Water droplets appearing on the outer surface of a glass tumbler containing cold water and ice cubes.
Dew drops on plants in the morning.
Water drops accumulating on the inner side of a steel plate used to cover a boiling utensil.
Atmospheric Water Generator (AWG) machines collecting water from humid air.

concepts.title: Melting and Freezing
concepts.definition: Melting is the conversion of a solid into a liquid state (usually on heating), as seen with ice turning into water or wax becoming liquid. Freezing is the conversion of a liquid into a solid state (usually on cooling), such as water turning into ice or liquid wax solidifying.
examples: Ice cube melting into water when left at room temperature (Activity 8.1).
A burning candle turning wax into liquid, which then solidifies when cooled.
Coconut oil solidifying during the winter season.
Water in a freezer being converted into ice cubes.

concepts.title: Cooling Effect of Evaporation
concepts.definition: Evaporation causes a cooling effect because water takes heat from its surroundings to change into vapour. This is why water in porous containers or surfaces feels cooler after some evaporation occurs.
examples: Water in an earthen pot (Matka) or a Surahi remains cold because water seeps through the surface and evaporates.
Making and using an electricity-free model of a pot-in-pot cooler (Activity 8.9).
Cooling sensation on the skin when sweat evaporates or when hand sanitiser is rubbed on hands.
Sprinkling water on a hot floor or roof during summer to impart a cooling effect.

concepts.title: Cloud Formation
concepts.definition: As water vapour rises (being lighter than air), it enters cooler layers of the atmosphere where it condenses around tiny dust particles to form tiny droplets. These floating droplets aggregate to form clouds.
examples: Tiny water droplets joining together to form bigger drops that eventually fall as rain.
Observing haziness in a bottle containing smoke particles and water when squeezed and released (Activity 8.10).

concepts.title: Water Cycle
concepts.definition: The circulation of water between the Earth's surface and the atmosphere through evaporation, condensation, and return as rain, hail, or snow is known as the water cycle.
examples: Water from oceans and rivers evaporating and returning to Earth as precipitation.
Labeling the movement of water between clouds, rivers, oceans, and groundwater (Activity 8.11).

concepts.title: Water Conservation
concepts.definition: Using water wisely and protecting it from pollution is essential because only a small portion of Earth's water is fit for use by humans, plants, and animals.
examples: Repairing leaking taps and avoiding wastage during daily activities.
Keeping rivers and other water bodies free from pollution.
Conserving 'Nature’s Treasures' by using water responsibly as population increases.

concepts.title: Stars and Constellations
concepts.definition: Stars are heavenly bodies that shine with their own light. Constellations are imaginary patterns or shapes formed by groups of stars. The International Astronomical Union (IAU) officially lists 88 constellations. In Indian astronomy, the term nakshatra is used to denote a certain star or group of stars.
examples: Orion (the Hunter) featuring the star Ardra (Betelgeuse) and three middle stars representing a hunter's belt (Activity 12.3).
Taurus (the Bull) which includes Krittika (Pleiades) and Rohini (Aldebaran).
Ursa Major (Saptarishi) containing the Big Dipper pattern.
Ursa Minor containing the Little Dipper and the Pole Star (Dhruva Tara).
Canis Major containing Sirius, the brightest star in the night sky (Activity 12.3).
Drawing connecting lines between stars to imagine patterns (Activity 12.1).

concepts.title: The Pole Star (Dhruva Tara)
concepts.definition: The Pole Star, or Polaris, is a star that appears stationary in the North direction in the Northern hemisphere, making it useful for finding directions. It is part of the Little Dipper pattern.
examples: Locating the Pole Star by imagining a straight line passing through the two stars at the end of the Big Dipper's cup (Activity 12.2).

concepts.title: Night Sky Watching and Light Pollution
concepts.definition: Night sky watching is the observation of celestial objects, best done from dark, open areas. Light pollution—excessive artificial light—reduces visibility. Dark sky reserves, like Hanle in Ladakh, are established to preserve dark skies for research.
examples: Waiting 30 minutes for eyes to adjust to darkness (Activity 12.5 context).
Using apps like Sky Map or Stellarium, or tools like the Himalayan Chandra Telescope at Mount Saraswati.
The Hanle Dark Sky Reserve (HDSR) in Ladakh being the first of its kind in India.

concepts.title: The Sun and its Energy
concepts.definition: The Sun is the closest star to Earth and an extremely hot spherical ball of gases. It is about 100 times bigger than Earth in diameter and is the main source of heat and light energy. The distance between the Sun and Earth is 150 million km (1 au).
examples: The Sun powers the Earth's climate, weather, winds, and water cycle.
Sunlight is essential for plants to grow through photosynthesis, providing oxygen to animals and humans.
The next nearest star, Proxima Centauri, is about 269,000 au away.

concepts.title: Planets of the Solar System
concepts.definition: A planet is a large, nearly spherical object that revolves around the Sun. The Solar System has eight planets: Mercury (Budha), Venus (Shukra), Earth (Prithvi), Mars (Mangala), Jupiter (Brihaspati), Saturn (Shani), Uranus, and Neptune.
examples: Venus (Morning/Evening Star) is brightest at dawn or dusk (Activity 12.4).
Mars (Red Planet) appears red due to its soil colour.
Earth (Blue Planet) appears blue from space because of water coverage.
Giant gaseous planets (Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune) have large flat ring-like structures.
Pluto, once called the ninth planet, is now classified as a dwarf planet after the 2006 IAU definition.

concepts.title: Natural Satellites and the Moon
concepts.definition: A satellite is an object that moves around a larger object. The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite. It is about a quarter the size of Earth and takes nearly 27 days to complete one revolution. It has no atmosphere, water, or life.
examples: Circular bowl-like structures called craters on the Moon's surface.
India's Chandrayaan missions (1, 2, and 3) to study the Moon.
The Vikram lander and Pragyan rover of Chandrayaan-3 soft-landing near the Moon's south pole on 23 August 2023.
National Space Day celebrated in India on August 23 every year.

concepts.title: Asteroids and Comets
concepts.definition: Asteroids are rocky, irregular objects revolving around the Sun, mostly between Mars and Jupiter (Asteroid Belt). Comets (Dhumaketu) are icy-rocky visitors from the outer regions made of dust, gases, rocks and ice.
examples: The evaporating ice of a comet as it approaches the Sun forms a long tail.
The famous Halley's Comet which appears every 76 years.
Tribal names for comets: 'Pucchya-Taro' (star with a tail) or 'Zendya-Taro' (star like a flag).

concepts.title: Our Solar System
concepts.definition: The Solar System is made up of the Sun, eight planets, their moons, and many smaller objects including asteroids and comets. All these objects shine by reflecting sunlight.
examples: Revolution: the movement of all these objects around the Sun.
The Sun being the hardest and heaviest object, holding the system together.

concepts.title: The Milky Way Galaxy (Akasha Ganga)
concepts.definition: A galaxy consists of millions to billions of stars. Our Solar System is a tiny part of the Milky Way Galaxy, which appears as a faint band of light across a very dark night sky.
examples: The Milky Way clearly visible from high-altitude dark areas like Ladakh.

concepts.title: The Universe
concepts.definition: The Universe includes all galaxies, stars, and space. Scientists continue to search for life elsewhere, particularly on exoplanets revolving around other stars.
examples: The ongoing scientific search for evidence of life beyond Earth.

concepts.title: Biodiversity and Interdependence
concepts.definition: Biodiversity refers to the variety of plants and animals found in a particular region. Living things are interdependent, meaning they rely on each other for food, shelter, and survival.
examples: Trees providing food and shelter to birds and monkeys.
Animals such as monkeys jumping from tree to tree and butterflies moving from flower to flower.
Birds mimicking unique chirps as an example of diversity in nature.
Observing different plants, insects, and birds during a nature walk (Activity 2.1).

concepts.title: Classification of Plants (Herbs, Shrubs, Trees)
concepts.definition: Plants are grouped based on their height, stem nature, and branching patterns. Herbs have soft green stems; shrubs have woody stems branching close to the ground; trees have thick hard stems branching higher up.
examples: Tomato (herb), Rose (shrub), and Mango (tree).
Climbers (e.g., money plant) and creepers (e.g., pumpkin) have weak stems needing support (Activity 2.4).

concepts.title: Leaf Venation (Reticulate and Parallel)
concepts.definition: Venation is the pattern of veins on a leaf. Reticulate venation is net-like on both sides of a middle vein; parallel venation has veins running parallel to each other.
examples: Hibiscus leaves show reticulate venation.
Banana and grass leaves show parallel venation (Activity 2.5).

concepts.title: Root Systems (Taproot and Fibrous Roots)
concepts.definition: A taproot system has one main root with smaller side roots. A fibrous root system consists of a bunch of similar-sized thin roots arising from the base of the stem.
examples: Mustard and hibiscus have taproots.
Common grass and wheat have fibrous roots (Activity 2.6).

concepts.title: Seed Types (Monocots and Dicots)
concepts.definition: Plants are classified as Monocots (seeds with one cotyledon) or Dicots (seeds with two cotyledons). Monocots typically have parallel venation and fibrous roots; Dicots have reticulate venation and taproots.
examples: Dicots: Chickpea (chana) and kidney beans, showing reticulate venation in leaves and taproots.
Monocots: Maize, wheat, and lemongrass, showing parallel venation and fibrous roots (Activity 2.8).
Plants like sadabahar (periwinkle) and hibiscus are dicots; grasses and banana plants are monocots.

concepts.title: Animal Movement and Habitats
concepts.definition: Animals are grouped based on how they move (flight, running, swimming) and the body parts they use (wings, legs, fins).
examples: Pigeons use wings to fly; fish use fins to swim (Activity 2.9).
Ants use legs for walking; goats use legs for walking and jumping.

concepts.title: Adaptations and Survival
concepts.definition: Adaptations are special features that enable plants and animals to survive in certain surroundings.
examples: Conical shape of deodar trees and sloping branches help snow slide off in cold mountains.
Camels in hot deserts (Rajasthan) have long legs with wide hooves for sandy terrain, dry dung, and don't sweat to conserve water.
Camels in cold deserts (Ladakh) have shorter legs for mountains, two humps that shrink in late winters when food is scarce, and grow long hair for winter.
Rhododendrons having shorter heights and smaller leaves on mountain tops to survive heavy winds (e.g., Nilgiris) vs taller in other regions (e.g., Sikkim).
Cactus plants with thick fleshy stems to store water in deserts.

concepts.title: Habitat (Terrestrial, Aquatic, and Amphibians)
concepts.definition: Habitat is the place where a plant or animal lives. Terrestrial habitats are land-based (forests, deserts); Aquatic habitats are water-based (oceans, lakes).
examples: Terrestrial: Camels in deserts, Lions in forests.
Aquatic: Whale and colourful fish in the ocean.
Amphibians: Frogs, which can live both on land and in water.

concepts.title: Conservation of Biodiversity
concepts.definition: The protection and restoration of habitats and species to prevent the loss of biodiversity due to human activities.
examples: Project Tiger (1973), Cheetah Reintroduction Project (2022), and Protected areas for Great Indian Bustards (Gujarat, Rajasthan, Maharashtra).
The 'Save Silent Valley' movement against a hydroelectric dam over the Kunthipuzha river in Palakkad, Kerala, led by common people.
Documenting and preserving biodiversity across the Indian subcontinent.

concepts.title: Sacred Groves
concepts.definition: Sacred groves are community-protected patches of undisturbed forests where no one is allowed to harm animals or cut trees.
examples: Serving as repositories of medicinal plants and local biodiversity treasures across India.

concepts.title: Pioneers in Biodiversity (Janaki Ammal and Salim Ali)
concepts.definition: Notable scientists who documented and preserved India's rich biodiversity.
examples: Janaki Ammal: Botanist and head of Botanical Survey of India; dedicated to environmental work and the Save Silent Valley movement.
Salim Ali: The 'Birdman of India' and Padma Vibhushan awardee; wrote a landmark 10-book series on birds of the Indian Subcontinent.
Salim Ali's role in preserving Keoladeo National Park (Rajasthan) and Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary (Karnataka).

concepts.title: Magnetic and Non-magnetic Materials
concepts.definition: Materials attracted to magnets are magnetic materials (e.g., iron, nickel, cobalt). Materials not attracted are non-magnetic materials (e.g., plastic, wood, glass). Naturally occurring magnets discovered in ancient times are known as lodestones.
examples: Iron nails, steel paper clips, and objects containing nickel or cobalt being attracted to a magnet.
Testing objects like erasers (rubber), plastic rulers, and pencils (wood) which do not stick to magnets (Activity 4.1).

concepts.title: Poles of a Magnet
concepts.definition: The regions of a magnet where the attraction is strongest are called poles: the North pole and the South pole. They always exist in pairs even in the smallest broken piece of a magnet; a single pole cannot exist.
examples: Iron filings sticking mostly to the ends of a bar magnet rather than the middle (Activity 4.2).
Magnets come in shapes like bar, cylindrical, horse-shoe, and ring, all having two poles.

concepts.title: Magnetic Compass and Finding Directions
concepts.definition: A freely suspended magnet always rests in the north-south direction because Earth behaves like a giant magnet. A magnetic compass is a circular box with a magnetized needle mounted on a pin that indicates directions.
examples: Suspending a bar magnet by a thread to observe it resting in the N-S direction (Activity 4.3).
The end of the compass needle pointing North is usually painted red for easier identification.

concepts.title: Matsya-yantra (Machchhyantra)
concepts.definition: An ancient Indian navigation device consisting of a magnetized fish-shaped iron piece placed in a vessel of oil, used by sailors to find directions long before modern compasses.
examples: Indian sailors using the 'fish-shaped instrument' (matsya-yantra) to navigate the seas (Do you know? section).

concepts.title: Making Artificial Magnets and Compasses
concepts.definition: Magnetism can be induced in an iron piece by repeatedly stroking it with one pole of a permanent magnet. A simple compass can be made using a magnetized needle and a cork.
examples: Magnetising an iron sewing needle by stroking it 30-40 times in one direction with a bar magnet (Activity 4.4).
Floating the magnetized needle through a cork in a bowl of water or oil to find directions.

concepts.title: Attraction and Repulsion (The Sure Test)
concepts.definition: Unlike poles attract, and like poles repel. Repulsion is the only 'sure test' to identify whether an object is a magnet, as both poles of a magnet will attract ordinary iron.
examples: Interaction between two magnetic cars speeded towards or away from each other based on pole orientation (Activity 4.5).
Using a marking-less magnet and observing repulsion to identify its poles with another marked magnet.

concepts.title: Magnetic Effect through Materials
concepts.definition: Magnetic force is capable of passing through non-magnetic materials like thin sheets of wood, glass, or plastic without much change in strength.
examples: Moving a magnet below a cardboard tray to lead steel balls through a maze (Activity 4.7).
Picking out a steel paper clip from water by moving a magnet outside the glass.

concepts.title: Proper Care and Storage of Magnets
concepts.definition: Magnets lose properties if heated, hammered, or dropped. They should be stored in pairs with unlike poles together, separated by wood, and with soft iron keepers across the ends.
examples: Storing bar magnets with unlike poles on the same side and iron pieces (keepers) across the ends.
Keeping magnets away from cassettes, mobile phones, remote controls, and gadgets (More to know! section).
Storing a horse-shoe magnet with a single iron keeper across its two poles.

concepts.title: Fun with Magnets and Applications
concepts.definition: Magnets can be used to create interactive toys and games, and they have important applications in technology like Maglev trains and medicine.
examples: Creating a 'Hopping Frog' toy using ring magnets and their repulsion property.
Leading steel balls through a maze or picking up paper clips from water without getting wet.
Maglev (Magnetic Levitation) trains that use magnets to float above tracks.

concepts.title: Common Characteristics of Living Beings
concepts.definition: Living beings are distinguished by features like movement, growth, nutrition, respiration, excretion, response to stimuli, reproduction, and death. Absence of any of these indicates a non-living thing.
examples: Pigeons and snails moving, growing, and responding to stimuli (Activity 10.1).
Plants like climbers winding around objects to show movement, unlike books or pencils.

concepts.title: Nutrition, Respiration, and Excretion
concepts.definition: Living beings need nutrition for growth. Respiration involves gas exchange (using stomata in plants). Excretion is waste removal (sweat, urine, or water droplets on leaves).
examples: Insectivorous plants like Drosera having saucer-shaped leaves with sticky hairs to trap insects for nutrition.
Tiny pores called stomata on leaves helping plants take air in and out.
Water droplets on grass and roses as a form of plant excretion.

concepts.title: Stimuli and Response
concepts.definition: A stimulus is any event that prompts a response. Examples include light, touch, heat, and gravity.
examples: Touch-me-not (Mimosa/Chhui-mui) folding leaves when touched.
Sleeping leaves of Amla (Indian gooseberry) folding after sunset.
Stepping on a thorn or touching a hot cup of tea.

concepts.title: Seed Germination and Essential Conditions
concepts.definition: Seed germination requires air and the right amount of water. Water softens the seed coat to help the embryo develop into a plant.
examples: Most seeds don't need light for germination, but Coleus and Petunia require light while Calendula and Zinnia need darkness.
Excess water preventing air from reaching seeds and inhibiting germination (Activity 10.2).

concepts.title: Growth Orientation in Plants
concepts.definition: Plants show biased movement during growth: roots grow downwards into the soil, while shoots grow upwards and towards sunlight.
examples: Shoot of an inverted plant bending upwards to seek light while the root bends downwards (Activity 10.3).

concepts.title: Jagadish Chandra Bose and the Crescograph
concepts.definition: Indian scientist (1858-1937) who proved plants sense and respond to stimuli. He invented the crescograph to measure plant growth speed and reactions to light, heat, and electricity.
examples: Demonstrating that plants are sensitive to their environment using specialized recording instruments.

concepts.title: Life Cycle of a Plant
concepts.definition: Stages from seed germination, maturity (flowering and fruiting), to seed production for the next generation.
examples: Following a bean plant's life cycle from a seed to the formation of pods with new seeds (Activity 10.4).

concepts.title: Life Cycle of a Mosquito
concepts.definition: A four-stage cycle: Egg, Larva, Pupa, and Adult. Larvae and pupae live in water and move to the surface to breathe air.
examples: Female mosquitoes transmitting diseases like malaria and dengue.
Spraying kerosene oil on stagnant water to create a layer that blocks air from larvae and pupae (Activity 10.5).

concepts.title: Life Cycle of a Frog
concepts.definition: Stages include spawn (jelly-like egg clusters), embryo, tadpole (aquatic with swimming tail), froglet, and adult frog.
examples: Tadpoles developing hind legs and losing their tails as they transform into land-dwelling adults (Activity 10.6).

concepts.title: Life Cycle of a Silk Moth
concepts.definition: Four-stage development (Egg, Larva, Pupa, Adult). Larvae (caterpillars) secrete silk fibers to form cocoons.
examples: Silk production centers set up by the Khadi and Village Industries Commission (KVIC) in India.

concepts.title: Objects and Materials
concepts.definition: A material is any substance used to create an object. Materials like clay and iron are used to make the 'crucible' (a vessel for melting substances), as mentioned in the ancient scientific text Rasaratnasamuchchaya.
examples: A tumbler can be made from plastic, glass, or metal (Activity 6.3).
Materials used to make a pen include plastic, metal, and ink.

concepts.title: History of Pottery and Terracotta
concepts.definition: Pottery in India dates back 7,000-8,000 years to Lahuradewa (Ganga plains) and Mehrgarh (Baluchistan). Terracotta refers to baked clay. Harappan (Sindhu-Sarasvati) pottery (2600-1900 BCE) featured black-on-red designs.
examples: Storage jars for grains and oil with geometric patterns exhibited at the National Museum, New Delhi.
Decorative coats on pottery known as 'slips'.

concepts.title: Classification of Materials
concepts.definition: Classification is the method of arranging objects into groups based on common properties (color, shape, material) to study patterns and behavior.
examples: Sorting balls by their bounce height (Activity 6.4).
Grocers keeping similar food items together for easy identification.

concepts.title: Appearance: Lustrous and Non-lustrous
concepts.definition: Lustre is the shiny appearance typical of metals like gold, copper, and aluminium. Materials that do not shine are non-lustrous.
examples: Freshly cut surface of iron showing lustre.
Materials becoming dull due to the effect of air and moisture (oxidation).

concepts.title: Hardness and Softness
concepts.definition: Hard materials are difficult to compress or scratch (stone, iron); soft materials are easily compressed (sponge, eraser). Hardness is a relative property.
examples: Rubber being harder than sponge but softer than iron (Activity 6.5).

concepts.title: Transparency: Transparent, Opaque, and Translucent
concepts.definition: Transparent materials allow clear vision (glass, air); opaque materials do not allow vision (wood); translucent materials allow hazy vision (frosted glass, butter paper).
examples: Hiding behind a wall (opaque) vs looking through a glass window (transparent) in hide-and-seek.

concepts.title: Solubility and Solutions
concepts.definition: Soluble materials (sugar, salt) completely disappear in water to form a solution. Insoluble materials (sand, sawdust) do not dissolve even after stirring.
examples: Mixing sugar, salt, and lemon juice in water to make Shikanji (lemonade) (Activity 6.7).
Oxygen gas dissolved in water supporting aquatic life.

concepts.title: Matter, Mass, and Volume
concepts.definition: Matter is anything that occupies space and has mass. Mass quantifies quantity (kg), while volume is the space occupied (L, mL, or SI unit m^3). Notation requires a space between the number and unit (e.g., 7 kg).
examples: Using a balance to weigh cups of water, sand, and pebbles to compare their mass (Activity 6.8).
Volume of water marked as 'net quantity' on bottles (e.g., 500 mL).

concepts.title: Ayurvedic Guna (20 Properties of Matter)
concepts.definition: Ayurveda (Ahtanga hridaya) describes all physical matter, living systems, and food through 20 properties (gunas) organized in 10 pairs of opposites.
examples: Guru (heavy) vs Laghu (light); Shita (cold) vs Ushna (hot); Mridu (soft) vs Kathina (hard); Drava (liquid) vs Sandra (solid).
Sthira (stable) vs Chala (moving/unstable); Snigdha (unctuous/oily) vs Ruksha (dry).

concepts.title: ORS — Oral Rehydration Solution
concepts.definition: A solution prepared at home or using packets to treat dehydration due to illness.
examples: Dissolving 6 teaspoons of sugar and half a teaspoon of salt in 1 litre of boiled and cooled water.

concepts.title: History of Measurement
concepts.definition: Ancient units like Angula (finger width), Handspan (Balisht), Stride, and Yojana varied between individuals. Harappan excavations reveal scales with ruled markings, indicating early standardized measurement.
examples: Measuring the classroom table using a handspan (balisht) as a unit (Activity 5.1).
Traditional carpenters still using angula for measurements.

concepts.title: Standard Units and SI System
concepts.definition: The International System of Units (SI) was adopted to ensure uniform measurements globally. The SI unit of length is the metre (m).
examples: Large lengths are measured in kilometres, while smaller lengths use centimetres or millimetres.

concepts.title: Metric Conversions (Length)
concepts.definition: Length units are written in lowercase (m, km, cm) with a space between the number and unit. Symbols are never pluralized (e.g., 5 kg, not 5 kgs).
examples: A 1.5 km distance between home and school is equivalent to 1500 metres.

concepts.title: Correct Measurement Techniques
concepts.definition: Accuracy requires placing the scale along the object, positioning the eye directly above the point, and adjusting for worn or broken scale ends.
formulae: Actual Length = Final Reading - Initial Reading (for broken ends)
examples: Subtracting 1.0 cm from a final reading of 10.4 cm to get 9.4 cm for a broken-end scale.
Using a thread or flexible tape to measure the girth of a tree or a curved line.

concepts.title: Position and Reference Point
concepts.definition: A reference point is a fixed object or location used to describe the position or distance of an object.
examples: Kilometre stones showing distance relative to 'Delhi' as the reference point.
Using a bus stand as a common reference point to compare house distances.

concepts.title: Rest and Motion
concepts.definition: An object is in motion if its position changes with respect to a reference point over time; otherwise, it is at rest. Motion is relative to the chosen reference point.
examples: Passengers in a bus are at rest relative to the bus but in motion relative to trees outside.

concepts.title: Types of Motion: Linear and Circular
concepts.definition: Linear motion involves movement along a straight line. Circular motion occurs when an object follows a circular path.
examples: An orange dropping from a tree or students in a march-past (linear).
A whirling eraser tied to a thread or a merry-go-round (circular).

concepts.title: Oscillatory and Periodic Motion
concepts.definition: Oscillatory motion is to-and-fro movement about a fixed position. Motion that repeats itself at regular intervals is called periodic motion.
examples: The movement of a swing or a vibrating metal strip (oscillatory).
Circular and oscillatory motions are also periodic as they repeat their paths.

concepts.title: Distance Measurement using Wheels
concepts.definition: Distance can be measured by multiplying the number of wheel rotations by the wheel's circumference.
formulae: Distance = Number of Turns x Circumference
examples: Using a Jones Counter attached to a bicycle wheel to measure road-running race distances.

chapters.title: Methods of Separation in Everyday Life

concepts.title: Handpicking
concepts.definition: A method of separating solid components from a mixture by hand based on differences in size, colour, or shape. Kabir's quote highlights discarding vices while keeping virtues, similar to winnowing or handpicking.
examples: Removing stones and husk from grains (wheat or rice).
Separating whole black peppers from pulao (Activity 9.1).

concepts.title: Threshing and Threshers
concepts.definition: Threshing is the process of beating harvested stalks against a hard wooden log to separate grains. Modern machines called threshers perform this task alongside winnowing.
examples: Separating grains from wheat stalks in Haryana fields (Activity 9.2).

concepts.title: Winnowing and Soop
concepts.definition: Winnowing uses wind or blowing air to separate heavier grains from lighter husk. A traditional bamboo tray called a soop is used for this process.
examples: Standing on a raised platform and moving a soop in the direction of wind to separate wheat from husk.

concepts.title: Sieving
concepts.definition: Sieving separates solid components of different sizes using a sieve. It is effective when particle sizes in a mixture vary significantly.
examples: Removing bran or small stones from wheat flour before kneading.
Construction sites using large sieves to separate pebbles and stones from sand.

concepts.title: Evaporation and Salt Production
concepts.definition: Evaporation converts liquid into vapour, leaving dissolved solids behind. This is the primary method for obtaining salt from seawater.
examples: Obtaining common salt from Sambhar Lake or seawater pits.
Drying medicinal herbs in the shade in Ayurveda to evaporate excess water.
Dandi March (Namak Satyagrah) as a struggle for salt production rights.

concepts.title: Sedimentation and Decantation
concepts.definition: Sedimentation involves the settling of heavier insoluble solids at the bottom. Decantation is the removal of the top liquid after sedimentation.
examples: Washing rice and pulses where stones settle and water is tilted out.
Gently pouring tea after letting tea leaves settle (without a strainer).

concepts.title: Filtration: Filtrate and Residue
concepts.definition: Filtration separates insoluble solids from a liquid using a filter (cloth, paper, or sand). The clear liquid is the filtrate, and the trapped solid is the residue.
examples: Using a strainer for tea or a piece of cloth for muddy water.
Folding filter paper into a cone for fine filtration (Activity 9.4).

concepts.title: Churning and Mathni
concepts.definition: Churning is the process of vigorous stirring used to extract lighter butter from curd (buttermilk). A mathni (churner) is used for this purpose.
examples: Lady using a big mathni to separate butter from churning curd in Madhya Pradesh.

concepts.title: Magnetic Separation
concepts.definition: Separation of magnetic substances (like iron) from non-magnetic ones using a magnet.
examples: Using magnets to pick up iron nails dropped in sawdust (Activity 9.12).
Industrial cranes with magnets separating scrap iron for recycling.

concepts.title: Environmental Impact of Separation
concepts.definition: Modern separation often involves awareness of pollutants like plastics that interfere with natural systems and ecosystems.
examples: Fisherman's net trapping plastics along with fish, highlighting river pollution.

chapters.title: Mindful Eating: A Path to a Healthy Body

concepts.title: Food Diversity and Traditional Dishes
concepts.definition: Food habits across Indian states (Punjab, Karnataka, Manipur) vary based on soil, climate, and locally grown crops.
examples: Sarson da saag and makki di roti in Punjab.
Idli, dosa, and ragi mudde in Karnataka.
Eromba and kangsoi in Manipur (Activity 3.2).

concepts.title: Culinary Practices and Tools
concepts.definition: Methods of food preparation have evolved from traditional tools like chulha and sil-batta to modern gas stoves and electrical grinders due to technological advancements.
examples: Interacting with elders to compare manual grinding versus modern electrical mixers (Activity 3.3).

concepts.title: Energy-Giving Foods (Carbohydrates and Fats)
concepts.definition: Carbohydrates (glucose, starch) provide instant energy, while fats serve as stored energy and keep the body warm.
examples: Sources of carbohydrates: wheat, rice, maize, and potatoes.
Sources of fats: groundnuts, almonds, ghee, and oil.
Polar bears surviving winter hibernation on accumulated body fat.

concepts.title: Body-Building Foods (Proteins)
concepts.definition: Proteins are essential for the growth and repair of the body. They are found in both plant and animal sources.
examples: Plant sources: pulses, beans, peas, and nuts.
Animal sources: milk, eggs, fish, and meat.
Edible mushrooms are also good protein sources.

concepts.title: Protective Nutrients and Vitamins
concepts.definition: Vitamins (A, B1, C, D) and minerals protect the body from diseases and maintain health even when required in small amounts.
examples: Vitamin C in citrus fruits (lemon, orange) cures scurvy (James Lind, 1746).
Vitamin A keeps eyes and skin healthy.

concepts.title: Deficiency Diseases and Minerals
concepts.definition: Lack of specific vitamins or minerals in the diet leads to deficiency diseases.
examples: Goitre (neck swelling) caused by iodine deficiency; prevented by iodised salt.
Calcium and Vitamin D required for strong bones (Rickets).

concepts.title: Dietary Fibre (Roughage) and Water
concepts.definition: Roughage helps in undigested food passage and smooth stools. Water aids nutrient absorption and waste removal via sweat and urine.
examples: Wholegrains, pulses, and leafy vegetables provide essential roughage.

concepts.title: Nutrition Research and PM POSHAN
concepts.definition: Coluthur Gopalan (1918-2019) initiated nutrition research in India, leading to the Mid Day Meal (PM POSHAN) initiative in schools.
examples: Analyzing over 500 Indian foods for their nutritional value.

concepts.title: Food Testing Protocols
concepts.definition: Simple chemical tests are used to detect starch, fats, and proteins in food samples.
formulae: Protein + Copper sulphate + Caustic soda = Violet colour
examples: Presence of starch in potato slices or boiled rice (Activity 3.5).
Leaving an oily patch on paper to test for fats (Activity 3.6).

concepts.title: Balanced Diet, Junk Food, and Millets
concepts.definition: A balanced diet contains all nutrients, roughage, and water. Junk food (high-calorie, low-nutrient) causes obesity. Millets (Nutri-cereals) are highly nutritious native crops. Food miles measure the distance from farm to plate.
examples: Comparing nutritional labels of potato wafers versus roasted chana (Activity 3.9).
FSSAI regulations and food fortification (adding nutrients during processing).
Promoting local food to reduce food miles and pollution.

concepts.title: Natural and Human-made Resources
concepts.definition: Resources from nature (air, water, forests) sustain life. Human-made resources (furniture, bicycles) are created for comfort. M.K. Gandhi's quote on need versus greed highlights the need for judicial use: "Earth provides enough to satisfy every man's need but not for every man's greed."
examples: Natural assets: Western Ghats, sunshine, fertile soil.
Human-made resources: Solar panels, furniture, electric bulbs.

concepts.title: Air: Composition and Wind Energy
concepts.definition: Air is a mixture of nitrogen (78%), oxygen (21%), and argon, carbon dioxide, and others (1%). Moving air is Wind, which powers windmills to generate electricity.
examples: Muppandal Wind Farm (Tamil Nadu), Jaisalmer Wind Park (Rajasthan), and Brahmanvel Wind Farm (Maharashtra).
Rotating wings of a firki (paper pinwheel) as a model for windmills (Activity 11.2).

concepts.title: Water: Sources and Conservation
concepts.definition: Two-thirds of Earth is covered by water, mostly saline (oceans). Freshwater (rivers, lakes, groundwater) is limited. World Water Day is observed on 22nd March.
examples: Fetching water over long distances in water-scarce regions.
Human activities like throwing plastic wrappers/trash pollute freshwater sources.

concepts.title: Rainwater Harvesting: Bawadi and Vav
concepts.definition: Rainwater harvesting is storing rainwater for later use. Traditional stepwells serve as systems to harvest filtered rain and water seepage.
examples: Bawadi in Rajasthan (e.g., Toorji ka Jhalra in Jodhpur) and Vav in Gujarat.

concepts.title: Solar Energy: The Primary Source
concepts.definition: The Sun provides heat and light, serving as the main source of energy for all living beings (photosynthesis, heat).
examples: Solar panels on street lights, solar cookers, and solar water heaters.
Traditional gratitude ritual of offering water to the Sun in the morning.

concepts.title: Forests: Preservation and Chipko Movement
concepts.definition: Forests provide products (fruit, wood, medicine) and shelter. Traditional conservation prevents plucking fruits left for wildlife (Activity 11.4).
examples: Van Mahotsav week in July for planting trees.
Chipko movement in Uttarakhand where women hugged trees to prevent felling.

concepts.title: Soil Formation and Agents
concepts.definition: Soil forms over thousands of years through rock disintegration by the Sun, water, and living organisms. Earthworms act as agents that turn and loosen the soil.
examples: Recycling in nature: fallen forest leaves decaying to enrich soil nutrients.

concepts.title: Rocks, Minerals and Ancient Tools
concepts.definition: Rocks are made of minerals from which metals (Gold, Al, Cu, Fe) are extracted. Rocks have been used for construction and tools for millennia.
examples: Roofing slates and laterite bricks.
Ancient tools: hand axes and arrowheads.
Basic mobile phones containing gold, silver, copper, and cobalt.

concepts.title: Fossil Fuels: Petroleum, Coal and Gas
concepts.definition: Non-renewable fuels (petrol, diesel, natural gas, coal) formed from ancient remains of plants and microorganisms over millions of years.
examples: CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) as a cleaner alternative for vehicles.
Coal used for generating most of India's electricity.

concepts.title: Renewable versus Non-renewable Resources
concepts.definition: Renewable resources (Air, Water, Forest) replenish within a reasonable time. Non-renewable (Minerals, Soil, Fossil fuels) do not.
examples: Walking or cycling instead of using fuel-based vehicles.

concepts.title: Judicious Consumption and Future Sustainability
concepts.definition: Conserving natural resources and using alternatives (Electric Vehicles) to ensure present needs are met without harming the future environment.
examples: Reducing food miles by supporting local producers to cut transport pollution.

concepts.title: Unreliability of Sense of Touch
concepts.definition: Experience shows that sense of touch is subjective and not always a reliable indicator of hotness or coldness.
examples: Dipping hands in warm, tap, and ice-cold water to compare subjective feel (Activity 7.1).

concepts.title: Temperature and the Thermometer
concepts.definition: Temperature is a reliable measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of an object. A thermometer is the device used for this measurement.
examples: A hotter body has a higher temperature than a colder one.

concepts.title: Clinical and Digital Thermometers
concepts.definition: Clinical thermometers measure human body temperature. Modern digital versions use heat sensors and avoid toxic mercury.
examples: Using a digital clinical thermometer with a reset button and beep sound (Activity 7.2).

concepts.title: Normal Body Temperature (37.0°C)
concepts.definition: The average body temperature of healthy people is taken as 37.0°C (98.6°F). It ranges from 35.0°C to 42.0°C.
examples: Healthy children often have slightly higher temperatures than adults; elderly people may have lower ones.

concepts.title: Mercury Toxicity and Infrastructure
concepts.definition: Mercury is toxic and difficult to dispose of safely if a glass thermometer breaks, leading to its replacement by digital and non-contact (Infrared) thermometers.
examples: Infrared thermometers used for remote screening during the COVID-19 pandemic.

concepts.title: Laboratory Thermometer and Range
concepts.definition: Used for measuring non-body temperatures (e.g., boiling water, ice). It typically ranges from -10°C to 110°C.
examples: Measuring the constant temperature of melting ice or boiling water (Activity 7.5).

concepts.title: Temperature Scales: Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin
concepts.definition: The three main temperature scales. Kelvin (K) is the SI unit of temperature.
examples: Converting Shillong's boiling water temperature (~98°C) to Kelvin.

concepts.title: Measurement Precautions and Smallest Division
concepts.definition: Thermometers must be held vertically and read while immersed. The smallest division depends on the marks between bigger units.
examples: A thermometer with 10 divisions between 0°C and 10°C has a smallest division of 1°C (Activity 7.4).

concepts.title: Air Temperature and Weather Reports
concepts.definition: Air temperature is a primary weather parameter monitored at weather stations. Daily reports include maximum and minimum temperatures.
examples: Analyzing 10 successive days of weather reports for temperature trends (Activity 7.7).

concepts.title: Anna Mani: The Weather Woman of India
concepts.definition: Indian scientist (1918-2001) who invented numerous weather measurement instruments and was a pioneer in solar and wind energy research.
examples: Her work helped India become a global leader in renewable energy.

concepts.title: Absolute Zero and Extreme Temperatures
concepts.definition: Absolute zero (-273.15°C or 0 K) is the lowest possible temperature. The Sun's core reaches 15 million degrees Celsius.
examples: Pulse rate was a traditional indicator of fever in India before thermometers.

concepts.title: Science and Curiosity
concepts.definition: Science is a way of thinking and observing to understand the universe. Curiosity is the most important element for scientific discovery.
examples: Wondering why stars shine or how flowers know when to bloom.

concepts.title: The Jigsaw Puzzle of Science
concepts.definition: Science is like an unending jigsaw puzzle. New knowledge adds pieces, and sometimes discoveries move pieces (change theories).
examples: New discoveries changing our understanding of previous theories or "wrongly placed puzzle pieces."

concepts.title: The Scientific Method: Five Steps
concepts.definition: A step-by-step process used to find reliable answers: Observation, Questioning, Guessing (Hypothesis), Testing (Experiment), and Analysis.
examples: Identifying why a pen stopped writing—guessing it is out of ink vs. dried ink (Activity 1.1).

concepts.title: Everyday Applications of Science
concepts.definition: Anyone who uses the scientific method to solve daily problems works like a scientist.
examples: A bicycle repairer finding a flat tyre leak; a cook wondering why dal spilled; an electrician diagnosing a bulb failure (Activity 1.2).

concepts.title: Collaboration and Long-term Learning
concepts.definition: Science is often a team effort. Not all answers are found at once, as learning science is an ongoing journey across different grades.
examples: Scientists working together in large teams across the world.

concepts.title: Spirit of Inquiry: Becoming a "Whys" Person
concepts.definition: The goal of science is to develop the habit of asking "Why?" and seeking how things work through keen observation. "After all, to be a wise person, you must be a 'whys' person!"
examples: Asking "Why?" about something and attempting to find an answer through observation (Activity 1.3).

concepts.title: Earth and Beyond
concepts.definition: Scientific curiosity extends from our home (Earth's plants and animals) to matters beyond (Sun, Moon, and Stars).
examples: Watching a caterpillar transform into a butterfly as an observation of Earth's diversity.
